title: JIT: Segfault from invalid memory read in `_PyTier2Interpreter`
author: devdanzin
body:
# Crash report

### What happened?

It's possible to segfault a JIT build by running the code below:

```python
def f1():
    a = "a"
    for i in range(50):
        x = a[i % len(a)]

    s = ""
    for _ in range(10):
        s += ""

    class A: ...
    class B: ...

    match s:
        case int(): ...
        case str(): ...
        case dict(): ...

    (
        u0,
        *u1,
        u2,
        u4,
        u5,
        u6,
        u7,
        u8,
        u9, u10, u11, 
        u12, u13, u14, u15, u16, u17, u18, u19, u20, u21, u22, u23, u24, u25, u26, u27, u28, u29,
    ) = [None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None,
         None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None,
         None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None,
         None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None,]

    s = ""
    for _ in range(10):
        s += ""
        s += ""

for i in range(11000):
    f1()
```

Backtrace:

    at Python/pythonrun.c:1366

Output from running with `PYTHON_LLTRACE=4 PYTHON_OPT_DEBUG=4`:
[3004_segfault_lltrace_opt_debug.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24306906/3004_segfault_lltrace_opt_debug.txt)

Found using [lafleur](https://github.com/devdanzin/lafleur/).


### CPython versions tested on:

CPython main branch

### Operating systems tested on:

Linux

### Output from running 'python -VV' on the command line:

Python 3.15.0a3+ (heads/main-dirty:3cc57505e53, Dec 23 2025, 05:19:47) [Clang 21.1.2 (2ubuntu6)]

<!-- gh-linked-prs -->
### Linked PRs
* gh-143124
<!-- /gh-linked-prs -->

comments.author: Fidget-Spinner
comments.body:
Thanks, verified. The problem is that the first instruction is a super instruction (`_BINARY_OP_INPLACE_ADD_UNICODE`). The progress requirement forces the trace recorder to record a deopt version of that (`BINARY_OP`). However, that leads to a wrong trace as the following _STORE_FAST is not recorded, as the trace recorder rightfully thinks that a super instruction has executed.

I could add a special case for super instructions in the trace recorder, but that would just be error-prone. I'm thinking of removing the progress requirements from the JIT and just make it so ENTER_EXECUTOR executes itself if `next_instr == this_instr`. WDYT @markshannon ?

comments.author: Fidget-Spinner
comments.body: The other benefit of removing the progress requirements is we get more speed -- the first instruction can now be specialized. The first instruction is actually a really important one too because it usually corresponds to a loop header!

comments.author: markshannon
comments.body:
First some thoughts on `_BINARY_OP_INPLACE_ADD_UNICODE`:

`_BINARY_OP_INPLACE_ADD_UNICODE` is a weird special case that only exists because of an historic promise that:
```
s = some_string
for _ in range(1000):
    s += "x"
```
would not be quadratic.


If we lower the JIT thresholds a bit (which I think we should anyway) we could do the optimization in the JIT and do away with the super-instruction.

comments.author: Fidget-Spinner
comments.body:
> If we lower the JIT thresholds a bit (which I think we should anyway) we could do the optimization in the JIT and do away with the super-instruction.

I don't think we can do that considering it will remove the quadratic optimization for non-JIT platforms/runtimes, which would be quite bad. Having quadratic behavior on interpreter and non-quadratic on JIT is a weird disparity that I don't want to introduce.

comments.author: markshannon
comments.body:
> I'm thinking of removing the progress requirements from the JIT ...

We need to be careful to  avoid infinite deopt loops that make no progress?

> just make it so `ENTER_EXECUTOR` executes itself if `next_instr == this_instr`

I presume you mean after we return from tier2. How would that work with tailcaliing in the future (when tier2 tailcalls into tier1)

comments.author: markshannon
comments.body:
> I don't think we can do that considering it will remove the quadratic optimization for non-JIT platforms/runtimes, which would be quite bad. Having quadratic behavior on interpreter and non-quadratic on JIT is a weird disparity that I don't want to introduce.

We don't need to drop it from the interpreter if there is no JIT. We would just don't perform that specialization if the JIT is enabled.

comments.author: Fidget-Spinner
comments.body:
> We don't need to drop it from the interpreter if there is no JIT. We would just don't perform that specialization if the JIT is enabled.

Ok fair enough.

comments.author: markshannon
comments.body:
Instead of handling the special case in `ENTER_EXECUTOR`, which doesn't really have enough context to do it properly, we should do it in the jitted code.

We already have several forms of tier2 -> tier1 transfers: exits, deopts, handle-pending-and-deopt, error.
Adding another one, exit-but-dont-reenter, wouldn't be too much effort.
We can then specialize the first uop, but use a different exit uop for it.

comments.author: Fidget-Spinner
comments.body:
How are you envisioning the deopt exit with guranteed progress? in an ideal world, we would use 1 tier 1 op called PROGRESS and execute that which would execute the ENTER_EXECUTOR's original opcode, but I don't want to use up another more tier 1 ops

I'd prefer we set a bit on the executor that tells it not to be entered, and an extra branch on ENTER_EXECUTOR costs slightly more, but should be negligible as we spend more time in the JIT
